sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Postnatal musculoskeletal review should assess recovery of mobility, load tolerance, abdominal wall symptoms, pelvic floor concerns, and readiness for caring, exercise, and work roles.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Back, pelvic, abdominal, or perineal discomfort after birth
- Difficulty lifting, carrying, feeding positions, or prolonged standing
- Reduced confidence returning to exercise or work
- Urinary leakage, heaviness, or abdominal doming may also be reported

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Escalate for heavy bleeding, fever, wound issues, calf swelling, severe depression, major neurological symptoms, or acute cardiorespiratory concerns
- Mode of delivery, obstetric trauma, and sleep deprivation influence recovery
- Not all postnatal symptoms are benign or self-limiting

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Screen immediate red flags and whether the parent can safely mobilise and care for self and baby
- Encourage pacing, progressive walking, and task sharing where possible
- Escalate if symptoms are severe, worsening, or medically concerning

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Review abdominal wall, pelvic floor symptoms, lifting tolerance, posture, and function in parenting and household tasks
- Provide staged return-to-exercise and return-to-work advice matched to delivery, healing, and symptom profile
- Refer for obstetric, GP, continence, or mental health review when the presentation is outside straightforward musculoskeletal recovery

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - Postnatal rehab planning should include sleep disruption, feeding demands, and caregiving load
